name: Disney Dreams: The Original Selves
description:
What happens when Disney characters fall asleep and meet the REAL people they were based on? Eight silly, respectful, rhyming dream-stories for five-year-olds in which Ariel meets the girl who became sea foam, Pocahontas meets the real Amonute who was only twelve, Sleeping Beauty meets a Viking warrior woman, and Aladdin meets the Syrian man who actually made up his story. Each dream is a gentle introduction to the richer, stranger, older versions of the stories kids already love — told in verse, with proper attribution to the original sources, and just enough silliness to keep a kindergartener giggling.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Vilhelm Pedersen's original illustrations for Hans Christian Andersen's fairy tales (1849) — the first and most authentic visual interpretations, public domain. Kay Nielsen's 'East of the Sun, West of the Moon' (1914) — luminous art nouveau fairy tale illustrations. Arthur Rackham's fairy tale work (1900s-1930s). Edmund Dulac's Arabian Nights illustrations (1907). Warwick Goble's 'Indian Myth and Legend' illustrations (1913). John Bauer's Scandinavian troll and forest illustrations (1907-1915).

sections.Who She Is: A tall, beautiful Powhatan woman with long black hair that blows in the wind even when there's no wind. She can talk to a raccoon named Meeko and a hummingbird named Flit. She paints with all the colors of the wind (which is a LOT of colors). She falls in love with a blond English man named John Smith approximately four seconds after meeting him.
sections.Her World: A Disney Virginia forest where every leaf glows golden, waterfalls pour in slow motion, and a 400-year-old talking willow tree named Grandmother Willow gives advice. The animals are helpful, the rivers are turquoise, and colonialism is just a misunderstanding between one grumpy governor and some very patient indigenous people.
sections.Her Superpower: Running off cliffs and diving perfectly into water. Also, understanding English immediately despite never having heard it before. Also also, wind management.
sections.What She Wants: To choose her own path — 'just around the riverbend.' (Spoiler: her path leads to a colonist.)

sections.Who She Was: A real girl named Amonute, also called Matoaka. 'Pocahontas' was her nickname — it meant something like 'playful one' or 'little mischief.' She was about TEN to TWELVE years old when John Smith arrived in 1607. She was the daughter of Wahunsenacah, the paramount chief of the Powhatan Confederacy — about 30 tribes, 15,000 people, living in Tsenacommacah (tidewater Virginia) for thousands of years.
sections.Her World: Real Tsenacommacah: longhouses of bent saplings covered in bark and mats, surrounded by cornfields, bean gardens, and fishing weirs. The Powhatan people were farmers, fishers, traders, diplomats. They had a complex government, seasonal ceremonies, and a cosmology centered on Ahone (the creator) and Okee (the spirit of danger and change). The rivers were real rivers, not turquoise. The forests were real forests, not golden.
sections.What Actually Happened: She was likely present at a ceremony involving John Smith that may have been a ritual adoption, not a rescue. Years later, she was kidnapped by English colonists, held for ransom, converted to Christianity, renamed 'Rebecca,' married to tobacco planter John Rolfe (NOT John Smith), taken to England as a living advertisement for the Virginia Company, and died there at about 21. She never came home.
sections.Attribution: The Mattaponi tribe maintains their own oral history of Amonute's story. The Pamunkey tribe received federal recognition in 2015 and continues to tell the Powhatan perspective. Both accounts differ significantly from English colonial records.

sections.Who She Is: A 16-year-old mermaid with red hair and a purple seashell top. She has a collection of 'thingamabobs' (forks, she uses them as combs) and 'whozits' and 'whatzits galore.' She is obsessed with the human world the way a kid is obsessed with whatever they can't have. She has a fish best friend named Flounder (who is NOT a flounder) and a crab manager named Sebastian (who has a Jamaican accent for reasons no one has explained).
sections.Her World: An underwater kingdom of sparkly coral ruled by her father King Triton, who carries a magic trident and has STRONG opinions about humans. Ariel's grotto is a cavern full of human objects she's salvaged from shipwrecks — a museum of longing. Above the surface: a vaguely Mediterranean coastal kingdom where a handsome prince plays the flute on his ship.
sections.Her Superpower: The best singing voice under the sea (which she trades for legs, because Disney in 1989 thought that was romantic). Also: befriending dangerous marine animals and surviving explosions.
sections.What She Wants: To be 'part of that world.' The human world. She wants MORE. (She gets legs, a prince, and permanent exile from her family. Hooray?)

sections.Who She Was: A nameless mermaid in Hans Christian Andersen's story 'Den lille Havfrue' (1837). She is quiet, thoughtful, and deeply lonely. She falls in love with a human prince she rescues from drowning. She trades her tongue (not just her voice — her actual tongue is cut out) for human legs, but every step feels like walking on knives. The prince marries someone else. The mermaid is given a knife to kill him (his blood would turn her back), but she can't do it. She throws herself into the sea and dissolves into foam.
sections.Her World: Andersen's sea is cold, dark, northern — the Baltic, not the Caribbean. The mermaid's palace is made of coral and amber, lit by phosphorescent fish. The human world above is not magical — it's ordinary, full of churches and bells. Andersen lived in Denmark, was desperately poor as a child, and was in love with a man named Edvard Collin who rejected him. The story is widely understood as Andersen writing about impossible, self-destroying queer love.
sections.What Actually Happened: She dissolves into sea foam — but Andersen gave her a strange epilogue. She becomes a 'daughter of the air,' a spirit who can earn a soul through 300 years of good deeds. Children's good behavior shortens her sentence; their bad behavior extends it. (Andersen really knew how to make kids feel guilty.)
sections.Attribution: Hans Christian Andersen, 'Den lille Havfrue,' published in Copenhagen, 1837. Andersen wrote 156 fairy tales. Most of them are sadder than you remember.

sections.Who She Is: A princess with golden hair who has exactly 18 lines of dialogue in her entire movie. She sings, she picks berries, she dances with a prince she meets in the woods ONE TIME, she pricks her finger on a spindle, and she sleeps. That's it. That's her whole deal. She is technically the lead of 'Sleeping Beauty' (1959) but the three fairies (Flora, Fauna, and Merryweather) and the villain Maleficent are all more interesting than she is, which everyone knows but nobody says out loud.
sections.Her World: A 14th-century European castle rendered in the style of medieval tapestries by artist Eyvind Earle — angular, geometric, stunningly beautiful. The forest is purple and gold. The castle is tall and narrow. Everything looks like a stained-glass window. It is the most visually gorgeous Disney film and the most narratively empty.
sections.Her Superpower: Sleeping. (Also, being so beautiful that a man she met once will fight a dragon to kiss her unconscious body. If that's a superpower.)
sections.What She Wants: To meet the man she danced with in the forest 'once upon a dream.' Her entire desire fits inside a three-minute waltz.

sections.Who She Was: A VALKYRIE — a chooser of the slain, a warrior woman who decided who lived and who died in battle. In Norse mythology (the Völsunga saga and the Poetic Edda), Brynhildr defied Odin by choosing the wrong warrior to win a battle. As punishment, Odin pricked her with a sleeping thorn and surrounded her with a ring of fire. Only a hero brave enough to ride through the flames could wake her. That hero was Sigurd (Siegfried), who swore to marry her. Then, through magic and trickery, Sigurd was made to forget Brynhildr and marry someone else. When Brynhildr discovered the betrayal, she didn't cry. She didn't wait for rescue. She killed Sigurd and then threw herself on his funeral pyre.
sections.Her World: The Norse mythological landscape: Asgard, Midgard, and the battlefield. Valkyries ride over the slain, choosing who goes to Valhalla (the hall of the honored dead) and who goes to Hel. The ring of fire surrounding Brynhildr is not a decorative curse — it's Odin's prison for a woman who defied him. The flames are punishment for female disobedience, and the man who crosses them must be worthy of the woman who burned.
sections.What Actually Happened: She woke up ANGRY. She swore oaths. She demanded justice. When justice was denied (through magical deception), she took revenge with her own hands and then chose her own death on her own terms. She is the opposite of Aurora in every way: powerful, furious, autonomous, and terrifying.
sections.Attribution: The Völsunga saga (13th century, Iceland), Poetic Edda (compiled 13th century from older sources), and the Nibelungenlied (c. 1200, Germany, where she appears as Brünnhilde). Richard Wagner used her story for his Ring Cycle operas (1876). She's been sleeping in stories for a thousand years — and she was never once passive.

sections.Who She Is: The daughter of Chief Tui on the island of Motunui. She's drawn to the ocean (literally — the ocean chose her as a toddler and keeps giving her shells and nudging her toward adventure). She's stubborn, brave, bad at sailing (at first), and has zero interest in romance. She rescues a narcissistic demigod named Maui from an island, survives a giant sparkly crab named Tamatoa, and saves the world by recognizing that the lava monster Te Kā is actually the wounded goddess Te Fiti.
sections.Her World: A vibrant Polynesian island surrounded by a reef, beyond which her father forbids anyone to sail (because her grandmother told stories). The ocean is alive and playful. The underworld (Lalotai) is bioluminescent and full of monsters. The ancestors appear as an armada of ghost ships — the great wayfinders who once crossed the Pacific by stars.
sections.Her Superpower: Wayfinding — reading the stars, the currents, and the waves to navigate without instruments. Also: stubbornness that could move mountains. Also also: recognizing a goddess even when she looks like a monster.
sections.What She Wants: To know who she is. 'I Am Moana' is her answer — she is her grandmother's stories, her ancestors' courage, and her own choice to go beyond the reef.

sections.Who They Were: Real people. Between roughly 3000 BCE and 1200 CE, Polynesian navigators crossed the largest ocean on Earth — a third of the planet's surface — in double-hulled canoes, guided by stars, wave patterns, cloud formations, bird flights, and the taste of the water. They settled Hawaii, Aotearoa (New Zealand), Rapa Nui (Easter Island), Samoa, Tonga, Fiji, Tahiti, and hundreds of other islands. It is the greatest feat of navigation in human history. They didn't have compasses. They didn't have maps. They had knowledge passed down through chants, stories, and practice.
sections.Their World: The Pacific Ocean — Te Moana-nui-a-Kiwa. Not 'empty' as European cartographers drew it, but full: full of islands, full of currents, full of people connected by voyaging routes as real as any highway. Each island culture is distinct: Hawaiian, Samoan, Tongan, Māori, Tahitian, Cook Islands Māori. Moana blends them. The real wayfinders were specific people from specific places.
sections.What Actually Happened: Nainoa Thompson of the Polynesian Voyaging Society revived traditional navigation in the 1970s-80s, learning from Mau Piailug, a Satawalese navigator from Micronesia who was one of the last people alive who could navigate by stars alone. The canoe Hōkūle'a has now sailed over 150,000 nautical miles. Thompson was a consultant on the film. The ancestors in Moana's vision are not fictional — they're a tribute to people who are still alive and still sailing.
sections.Attribution: Polynesian oral traditions across many island nations. The Polynesian Voyaging Society (founded 1973, Hawaii). Mau Piailug (1932-2010, Satawal, Federated States of Micronesia) — the master navigator who shared his knowledge so it would not die. Nainoa Thompson (b. 1953, Hawaii) — who learned, practiced, and taught it forward.

sections.Who She Is: Queen of Arendelle. Has ice powers she can't control (until she can). Built an entire ice palace in one musical number while throwing away her gloves and letting her hair down, which is the most relatable thing any Disney character has ever done. In Frozen 2, she hears a mysterious voice, follows it north, discovers her mother was indigenous (Northuldra/Sámi), and becomes the protector of the enchanted forest instead of ruling the kingdom. She's the first Disney 'princess' whose story is NOT about romance — it's about self-acceptance and then, in the sequel, about ancestry.
sections.Her World: Arendelle: a Norwegian-inspired fjord kingdom with cute architecture and persistent winter problems. In Frozen 2: the Enchanted Forest, where the Northuldra people and Arendellian soldiers have been trapped behind a magical mist for 34 years because Elsa's grandfather built a dam designed to weaken the Northuldra's land. (Yes, Disney made a reparations movie.)
sections.Her Superpower: ICE. Snowflakes, ice castles, frozen fjords, sentient snowmen (Olaf), and a bridge made of crystallized water that she runs across while the ocean crashes beneath her. Also: a voice that can shatter magical barriers.
sections.What She Wants: In Frozen 1: to be left alone. In Frozen 2: to find out who's calling her and why she has powers. The answer: she's the bridge between the human and spirit worlds because her mother was Northuldra.

sections.Who She Was: In Hans Christian Andersen's 'Snedronningen' ('The Snow Queen,' 1844), the Snow Queen is NOT the heroine — she's the antagonist. She kidnaps a boy named Kai after a shard of a cursed mirror lodges in his eye and heart, making him see only ugliness and feel only coldness. She takes him to her palace of ice at the North Pole, where he sits frozen, trying to spell the word 'eternity' with ice shards. The HEROINE is Gerda — Kai's best friend, a brave girl who walks barefoot across the world to find him. Gerda, not the Snow Queen, is the original Elsa: a girl whose love melts ice.
sections.Her World: Andersen's Snow Queen lives in a palace of driving snow where 'the Northern Lights are the candles.' The journey to reach her passes through a robber girl's camp, a Sámi woman's hut (Andersen called her a 'Lapp woman,' using the term common in his era), and a Finnish woman who knows magic. The Sámi connection in Frozen 2 is actually in the ORIGINAL — Andersen put a Sámi woman in the story in 1844. Disney just took 169 years to center her.
sections.What Actually Happened: Gerda saves Kai with her tears. Not a kiss — tears. Her grief melts the ice in his heart. He cries too, and the mirror shard washes out. They walk home holding hands, and when they arrive, they discover they've grown up during the journey. The story ends with a Bible verse about becoming like children to enter the kingdom of heaven. It's a story about growing up, losing wonder, and finding it again through love that is NOT romantic — it's the love of childhood friendship.
sections.Attribution: Hans Christian Andersen, 'Snedronningen,' published in Copenhagen, 1844. Seven chapters, each a mini-adventure. It's Andersen's longest fairy tale and one of his most structurally ambitious — a quest narrative with a female hero that predates most of the adventure stories boys were given.

sections.Who He Is: A Mexican boy who loves music in a family that has BANNED music for four generations. His great-great-grandfather Héctor left to be a musician and (the family believes) never came back. Miguel has a secret guitar, practices in a hidden attic shrine to Ernesto de la Cruz (a famous dead singer), and on Día de los Muertos accidentally falls into the Land of the Dead. He's about 12, resourceful, earnest, and accompanied by a stray Xolo dog named Dante who turns out to be his spirit guide.
sections.Her World: Santa Cecilia, a Mexican town where the Rivera family makes shoes. The Land of the Dead is a neon-bright vertical city built on top of itself — a marigold-colored, music-filled afterlife where the dead continue to exist as long as someone living remembers them. Marigold petals are the bridge. Photos on ofrendas are the passport. Forgetting is the final death.
sections.His Superpower: Playing guitar with incredible soul for a kid who learned by watching YouTube. Also: the ability to cross between worlds (because he stole a dead man's guitar on Día de los Muertos, which is NOT recommended).
sections.What He Wants: To play music. Then: to be remembered. Then: to make sure HÉCTOR is remembered, because being forgotten is worse than being dead.

sections.Who She Was: The Aztec/Nahua goddess who presided over Mictlán, the underworld, alongside her husband Mictlāntēcutli (Lord of the Dead). She was born (in some versions, sacrificed as an infant) and sent to the underworld to rule the bones of the dead. She is depicted with a flayed face or a skull face, not because she's scary but because in Nahua cosmology, the dead are not frightening — they are family. The bones of the dead are seeds. Mictēcacīhuātl guards them so they can be replanted — which is literally what happens in the Aztec creation myth, where Quetzalcoatl steals bones from Mictlán and sprinkles them with blood to create new humans.
sections.Her World: Mictlán is not hell. It has nine levels, and reaching the deepest layer takes four years of traveling through wind, jaguars, mountains that crash together, and rivers of blood. But it's not punishment — it's just the journey every soul makes. The Aztec afterlife destination depended not on how you LIVED but on how you DIED: warriors and women who died in childbirth went to the sun; drowned people went to Tlalocan (rain god's paradise); everyone else traveled to Mictlán.
sections.What Actually Happened: The Spanish conquest (1519-1521) destroyed most of the Aztec religious infrastructure, but Mictēcacīhuātl survived — transformed. The month-long Aztec festival of the dead was compressed by Spanish missionaries into All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day (November 1-2), and Mictēcacīhuātl became the folk figure La Muerte or La Catrina. The ofrendas, marigolds, sugar skulls, and pan de muerto of modern Día de los Muertos are syncretic descendants of Nahua death rituals and Catholic observance. She's still being honored every November, 500 years later. The bones are still seeds.
sections.Attribution: Aztec/Nahua mythology, recorded (partially, and through colonial filters) in sources including the Codex Borgia, Bernardino de Sahagún's 'Florentine Codex' (1576), and oral traditions maintained by Nahua communities in central Mexico. La Catrina was drawn by José Guadalupe Posada (c. 1910) and named by Diego Rivera.

sections.Who He Is: A 'street rat' (his words) in the fictional city of Agrabah (rhymes with nothing real). He steals bread, has a pet monkey named Abu, and dreams of being more than his circumstances. He finds a magic lamp in a Cave of Wonders, befriends the Genie (a manic blue entity voiced by Robin Williams doing approximately 47 impressions per minute), disguises himself as 'Prince Ali,' and wins Princess Jasmine by... lying to her. He eventually tells the truth. Everything works out.
sections.Her World: Agrabah: a fictional Arab city that's simultaneously Baghdad, Cairo, Marrakech, and nowhere. Giant palace, bustling marketplace, desert dunes, and a cave full of treasure guarded by a sand-tiger head. The 'good' characters sound American; the 'bad' characters sound vaguely Middle Eastern. The guards are big and dumb. The merchants are sly. Every stereotype has a turban.
sections.His Superpower: Parkour (before parkour existed). Also: finding magical things in caves. Also: being likeable enough that a universe-powered entity does his homework for him.
sections.What He Wants: To be seen as more than a street rat. 'They're gonna know... Prince Ali!' But the real lesson is: be yourself. (The movie takes 90 minutes to reach a conclusion a kindergartener could tell you in five seconds.)

items.name: Hanna Diyab (The Storyteller Who Made Aladdin)

sections.Who He Was: A real man. Hanna Diyab was a Maronite Christian from Aleppo, Syria, born around 1688. In 1709, he traveled to Paris with a French traveler named Paul Lucas. There, he met Antoine Galland, who was translating 'One Thousand and One Nights' into French. Galland had run out of stories from the Arabic manuscript. Diyab told him several new ones — including 'Aladdin' and 'Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves.' Galland wrote them down, published them as part of the Nights, and never credited Diyab. The most famous 'Arabian' stories in the world were invented by a Syrian teenager telling tales to a Frenchman in Paris.
sections.His World: Aleppo in the early 1700s: one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth, a major stop on the Silk Road, famous for its covered souks, its citadel, and its diverse population of Arabs, Armenians, Kurds, Turks, and Maronite Christians like Diyab. The real 'Agrabah' was Aleppo — specific, real, ancient, sophisticated. Diyab wrote his own memoir (discovered in the Vatican Library in the 1990s) describing his travels from Aleppo to Paris and back. It's a picaresque adventure more interesting than anything in the Disney movie.
sections.What Actually Happened: Diyab set his Aladdin story in CHINA, not Arabia — a magical faraway land from Diyab's Aleppine perspective. The Genie in Diyab's version is terrifying, not funny — a powerful djinn bound to the lamp who grants wishes with dangerous precision. Galland polished Diyab's oral tales into literary French, and the stories entered European literature as 'authentic' Arabian Nights. They're not. They're one young Syrian man's imagination, sparked by the cosmopolitan world of Aleppo and the experience of being a stranger in Paris.
sections.Attribution: Hanna Diyab (c. 1688–c. 1763), oral storytelling to Antoine Galland, Paris, 1709. Diyab's memoir: 'D'Alep à Paris: Les pérégrinations d'un jeune Syrien au temps de Louis XIV,' manuscript in Vatican Library (Sbath 254), published in Arabic by Jérôme Lentin (2015) and in English translation by Paulo Lemos Horta as 'The Book of Travels' (2021). The best book about this: Paulo Lemos Horta, 'Aladdin: A New Translation' (2019).

sections.Who He Is: A boy in red underpants raised by wolves in the Indian jungle. He thinks he's a wolf until the tiger Shere Khan returns and the pack decides Mowgli must go to the 'man-village' for safety. He doesn't want to go. Along the way he meets Baloo the bear (who teaches him 'The Bare Necessities'), Bagheera the panther (who is Responsible), King Louie the orangutan (who wants the secret of fire), and Kaa the snake (who wants to eat him). In the end, he sees a girl from the village and follows her in, apparently forgetting all his animal family.
sections.Her World: A Disney jungle that's warm, bouncy, and full of singing animals. The trees are bendy, the rivers are friendly, and the most dangerous thing is an orangutan's jazz number. India is never mentioned by name. The human village appears only as a destination — thatched roofs, a girl with a water pot, and the implication that civilization is where you inevitably end up.
sections.His Superpower: Being adorable enough that apex predators adopt him instead of eating him. Also: rhythm, apparently.
sections.What He Wants: To stay in the jungle. (He doesn't get what he wants. He gets a girl instead. Disney, 1967.)

sections.Who They Are: There is no single 'original Mowgli' — Kipling invented him. But the children who actually grow up in India's forests are real: the Adivasi peoples — India's indigenous tribal communities who have lived in and with forests for thousands of years. The Baiga, Gond, Bhil, Santhal, Munda, and many other Adivasi groups don't see the forest as 'jungle' (wild, dangerous, needing to be left behind). The forest IS their home, their pharmacy, their temple, and their school. An Adivasi child doesn't need to choose between the forest and the village — they live in both.
sections.Their World: India's forests: the Sal forests of central India, the Western Ghats, the Sundarbans mangroves, the northeastern hills. Adivasi communities practice shifting cultivation (jhum), forest gardening, and hunting-gathering — not because they're 'primitive' but because these are sophisticated ecological management systems refined over millennia. The Bishnoi community of Rajasthan gave their lives to protect trees in 1730 (363 Bishnois were killed by the king's men while hugging trees — the original tree-huggers). The sacred groves maintained by Adivasi communities across India are some of the most biodiverse spots on Earth.
sections.What Actually Happened: British colonialism destroyed Adivasi forest rights. The Indian Forest Acts (1865, 1878, 1927) classified forests as government property, criminalizing Adivasi lifestyles that had sustained these forests for centuries. Kipling wrote 'The Jungle Book' in this context — an English boy 'mastering' the Indian jungle is a perfect colonial metaphor. Today, Adivasi communities fight for land rights through the Forest Rights Act (2006), which recognizes their historical relationship with forests. The struggle is ongoing.
sections.Attribution: Adivasi communities across India. The Bishnoi community (Rajasthan). Verrier Elwin (1902-1964), British-born anthropologist who lived with Adivasi communities and advocated for their rights. The Forest Rights Act of 2006 (Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers Act). Survival International and other organizations supporting Adivasi land rights.

sections.The Dreamscape: A riverbank at twilight — half Disney-painted, half real Virginia woodland. One side glows golden and purple like the movie. The other side smells like real mud and pine sap. A firefly bridge floats between them. Grandmother Willow is there, but she's quieter than usual.
sections.The Dream:
Pocahontas fell asleep beneath a painted sky,
Where digital stars don't blink — they just stand by.
She dreamed she stood beside a river, wide and brown,
And there upon the bank, a girl sat down.

The girl was small. Like, REALLY small. Like ten.
She had a stick, and she was poking at a wren.
"Who are you?" asked Pocahontas, tall and grand.
The girl looked up and didn't understand.

"I'm Amonute," she said. "Some call me Matoaka.
I'm playing. What's your name? You talk like you're from far away-ka."
"I'm... you," said Pocahontas. "I'm the movie version."
"The WHAT version?" said the girl with great aversion.

"I sing to willow trees. I dive off waterfalls.
I saved John Smith from—" "WAIT. John Smith? That man who tells tall tales?
He's smelly and he's old! He's twenty-SEVEN!
I'm TEN. That's gross. That's not the road to heaven."

Pocahontas blushed. (She'd never blushed before —
In movies, princesses don't blush, they SOAR.)
"I... painted with the colors of the wind?"
"That's not a thing," said Amonute, and grinned.

"But I can show you how we plant the corn.
Three sisters — corn and beans and squash — since I was born.
And I can show you how we weave a fish trap tight,
And how the stars look different every night.

My father rules the Powhatan — that's thirty tribes!
He keeps the peace with gifts and talks and sometimes bribes.
We're not just sitting here in feathers, waiting for
Some English boats to show up at our shore."

"But in my movie—" "Movies aren't real," she said.
She scratched the wren. It flew up past her head.
"But I am. And I'm tired. And I'm ten.
I want to play, and then play once again.

So please don't make me kiss a colonizer?"
Pocahontas laughed — the sound was a surpriser.
"I think," she said, "I think I'd like to play."
And so they skipped rocks till the dream gave way.
sections.What She Learned: That the real Pocahontas was a kid — a little kid who played and poked at birds and wasn't anybody's love interest. That the Powhatan people weren't waiting to be discovered — they were farming, governing, fishing, and living full lives. That 'Pocahontas' was a nickname meaning 'playful one,' and the real story is sadder and bigger than any movie.
sections.Source Note: Amonute/Matoaka was a real person of the Powhatan Nation, c. 1596-1617. The Mattaponi and Pamunkey tribes maintain their own accounts of her life. This dream-poem is fiction honoring her memory.

items.name: Ariel Dreams of the Sea-Foam Girl

sections.The Dreamscape: The ocean floor, but divided: one half is Disney-bright (neon coral, singing fish, a crab conducting an orchestra) and the other half is Andersen-dark (Baltic cold, pale jellyfish, blue-green silence). Ariel's grotto of thingamabobs merges into a quiet, empty cavern. A girl sits in the cave, transparent as glass.
sections.The Dream:
Ariel was sleeping in her grotto, cozy-tight,
Surrounded by her dinglehoppers in the night.
She dreamed she swam into a cave of cold,
Where sat a girl — see-through, and very old.

Not OLD-old. More like... faded. Like a song
You half-remember, humming all day long.
"Hello," said Ariel. "What's your name? I'm me!"
The faded girl just rippled, like the sea.

"I had a name once. I don't anymore.
I gave my tongue away. It made me sore."
"Your TONGUE?" gasped Ariel. "I just gave my voice!
I got it back, though. It was a bad choice."

"I didn't get mine back," the girl said, low.
"I walked on knives. Each step. You didn't know?
In my story, legs don't come for free —
They hurt like broken glass, from knee to knee."

Ariel sat down. (She had her tail right now.)
"What happened with your prince?" she asked. "And how—"
"He married someone else." The girl went dim.
"And I turned into foam. I couldn't swim.

I couldn't swim! A MERMAID turned to foam!"
She laughed, and Ariel felt very far from home.
"That's... that's the worst ending I've ever heard."
"It's real, though," said the girl. "That's Andersen's word.

The man who wrote me? He was sad inside.
He loved someone who said no. So... I died.
He put his heartbreak in a mermaid's tail.
I am a love letter that became a whale
Of sadness." (She was mixing metaphors.
She'd been sea foam for years. Cut her some slack, of course.)

"But YOU got legs AND voice AND prince AND cake!"
"There WAS no cake," said Ariel. "But... for your sake,
I wish you'd gotten more." The foam-girl smiled.
"You got the happy ending. I'm the child

Who WROTE the question: what would you give up for love?"
"Everything," said Ariel. "Stars above."
"I know," the foam-girl said. "I gave it all.
The answer is: some prices are too tall."

And as the dream dissolved, the foam-girl whispered clear:
"Don't give your voice for ANYONE, my dear."
sections.What She Learned: That the original Little Mermaid didn't get a happy ending — she dissolved into sea foam because the prince married someone else. That Hans Christian Andersen wrote the story from his own heartbreak. That giving up your voice is never, ever, ever a good trade. That the 'happy ending' version is newer and rarer than the sad one.
sections.Source Note: Hans Christian Andersen, 'Den lille Havfrue' (The Little Mermaid), 1837, Copenhagen. Andersen's unrequited love for Edvard Collin is documented in his letters and diaries.

sections.The Dreamscape: A ring of fire on a Norse mountaintop. Inside the ring: a bed of shields, not silk. Outside the ring: Disney's purple-and-gold forest, softly fading. The fire crackles with real heat. Aurora's three fairy godmothers hover nervously at the edge, arguing about whether the fire should be blue or pink.
sections.The Dream:
Aurora slept. (She's very good at that.)
She'd been asleep since page one, paragraph, flat.
She dreamed she walked into a wall of flame
And found a woman sleeping — but not the same.

This woman wore ARMOR. A breastplate! A SWORD!
Her muscles were enormous. (Not a word
You'd use for Disney princesses, you know.)
She snored like thunder. Then she stubbed her toe

On her own shield and woke up with a ROAR.
"WHO DARES—" She saw Aurora. "Who are you? What for?"
"I'm Sleeping Beauty?" Aurora said, unsure.
Brynhildr squinted. "You don't look very dure."

"Dure?" "It means tough. It's OLD. I'm very OLD.
I'm Brynhildr. I'm a VALKYRIE." She rolled
Her shoulders, cracked her neck, and drew her sword.
"I CHOSE who lived and died. That's not a word

You say in Disney movies, is it, dear?"
Aurora shook her head. She felt sincere fear.
"In MY story," Brynhildr said, and sat,
"Odin put me to sleep. He was a brat.

I picked the wrong guy to win the fight —
Well, I picked the RIGHT guy. Odin's might
Decided I was wrong. He stuck a thorn
In my side, and I slept. But I was BORN

To ride above the battlefield and CHOOSE.
I wasn't born to prettily just snooze."
"But..." Aurora said, "a prince came? And a kiss?"
Brynhildr laughed so hard she almost hissed.

"A man rode through the fire. Sigurd was his name.
I woke up, and I said: 'what is YOUR game?'
I made him SWEAR an oath before I'd budge.
I didn't flutter. Smile. I didn't fudge.

And THEN — he broke his oath. He was bewitched.
He married someone else. My whole life switched."
"What did you do?" asked Aurora, very small.
"I had him killed," said Brynhildr. "Then I held a ball.

Just kidding. Then I jumped into his fire.
I chose my death. I was no one's desire
To manage. I was MINE." She looked at the princess.
"Eighteen lines? In your whole movie? That's a finesse

Of... nothing. You're a painting, not a soul."
Aurora's lip wobbled. Then she felt something whole
Move through her like the fire. "But what if I
Woke UP? Not from a kiss. From MY OWN cry?"

Brynhildr grinned. "Now THAT'S a Valkyrie.
Now wake yourself. You don't need him. Or me."
sections.What She Learned: That the original sleeping woman was a VALKYRIE — a warrior who chose who lived and died in battle. That she was put to sleep as PUNISHMENT for disobedience, not because she touched a spindle. That she woke up angry, not grateful. That she took revenge when she was betrayed. And that maybe the most powerful thing a sleeping princess can do is wake herself up.
sections.Source Note: Brynhildr appears in the Völsunga saga (13th century, Iceland), the Poetic Edda's 'Sigrdrífumál' (Song of the Victory-Bringer), and the German Nibelungenlied (c. 1200). She is one of the most complex female characters in medieval European literature.

sections.The Dreamscape: The open Pacific Ocean at night. No island in sight. The stars are EVERYWHERE — not movie stars (twinkly, decorative) but NAVIGATION stars (specific, named, mapped into a mental compass). A double-hulled canoe rocks gently on the swell. The Disney ocean tries to make a cute wave-hand, but the real ocean is too big and too serious for that.
sections.The Dream:
Moana was dreaming on a real canoe —
No Disney ocean waving peek-a-boo.
The water here was black and deep and cold.
The stars were fierce. The wind was very bold.

A man sat at the stern. He wasn't Maui.
No tattoos talked. No hook went kablooey.
He wore a simple cloth. His eyes read sky
The way you'd read a book — from left to right to high.

"Who are you?" Moana asked. She wasn't scared.
(She'd fought a lava monster. She was prepared.)
"My name doesn't matter here," the man said low.
"I'm one of thousands. What I know, I know

From my grandfather, who learned it from his mother,
Who learned it from HER mother, from another,
Going back three thousand years of reading waves."
"Like me!" said Moana. "I learned in my movie! Braves

The ocean! Reads the stars!" He smiled — just barely.
"You learned it in a movie. That's done rarely
In ninety minutes. We learn it in a lifetime.
This star—" he pointed up — "that's not a rhyme

Or metaphor. That star is Hokupa'a.
We call it the fixed star. It sits right THAH."
(He pointed at the North Star.) "It doesn't move.
Everything else spins around it. That's the groove.

Now — feel the swell beneath us? Feel that bump?
That wave bounced off an island. Every lump
And roll and ripple in the water TELLS
You where land is. The ocean speaks in swells."

Moana closed her eyes and felt the sea.
"I feel it! It's like... a heartbeat!" "It might be,"
The navigator said. "My teacher, Mau,
Could feel an island fifty miles from now.

Mau Piailug. From Satawal. He was real.
He taught us what no movie makes you feel:
That the canoe does not move — the ISLANDS come to YOU.
You sit still in the center, and the world sails through."

Moana's eyes went wide. "That's beautiful."
"That's physics," said the man. "And it's dutiful —
We didn't cross the ocean just because.
We crossed it because our ancestors' cause

Was FINDING. Finding islands. Finding home.
Not one girl and a demigod, alone.
It took VILLAGES. Whole families sailed.
Babies were born at sea. Some people failed.
Some canoes were lost. That's real. That's the cost."

The stars turned slowly. Nothing else was tossed
Or sparkly or convenient or fun.
Just ocean. Stars. A canoe. And everyone
Who ever sailed by starlight, on this sea,
Sitting with Moana, wild and free.
sections.What She Learned: That Polynesian navigation is REAL and still practiced. That Mau Piailug was a real navigator who taught Nainoa Thompson. That the Hōkūle'a has sailed over 150,000 miles by star navigation. That it took villages, not individuals. That the canoe 'stays still' and the islands come to you — a completely different way of understanding movement. And that her movie was inspired by these real people, who deserve to be known by name.
sections.Source Note: The Polynesian Voyaging Society (founded 1973, Honolulu). Mau Piailug (1932-2010), Satawalese master navigator. Nainoa Thompson (b. 1953), Hawaiian navigator who revived traditional wayfinding. The Hōkūle'a canoe, launched 1975, has completed multiple worldwide voyages using traditional navigation.

sections.The Dreamscape: An ice palace — but WHICH ice palace? Elsa's glittering geometric creation merges with Andersen's Hall of Infinite Emptiness, where the Northern Lights flicker like cold candles. In the center, a small girl in bare feet stands on the frozen floor, not shivering. She's been walking for a very long time.
sections.The Dream:
Elsa dreamed of ice (she always does),
But this ice wasn't hers. It crackled. Buzzed.
A tiny girl stood barefoot on the floor.
She looked at Elsa. "I've been here before."

"Who ARE you?" Elsa said. "You'll catch a cold!"
"I'm Gerda," said the girl. "I'm very bold.
I walked from Denmark to the North Pole. Barefoot. Me.
To save my friend Kai from the Snow Queen's decree."

"The Snow Queen... that's... that's ME, right?" Elsa frowned.
"No, no," laughed Gerda. "You're the wrong way round!
In MY story, the Snow Queen is the BAD one.
She STEALS a boy. She's cold and mad — a sad one.

But YOU'RE the girl who learned to love her ice.
In MY story, I'M the hero. Isn't that nice?
I walked through ROBBER CAMPS — with actual robbers!
A reindeer helped me. (Reindeer are great job-ers.)

I visited a Sámi woman in a hut."
"A Sámi woman?!" Elsa's brain went — what?
"In YOUR story? Andersen's? From 1844?"
"Oh yes. She's in chapter five. She knows the score.

She didn't have a name. (That's Andersen's fault.)
But she was wise. She heated up some malt.
And told me: 'Gerda's power is in her heart.
She doesn't need magic — she's been strong from the start.'"

Elsa sat down hard upon her ice.
"You mean the Sámi were in the story? Twice?
Once in his, and once in mine? But no one SAID—"
"Adults forget things," Gerda shook her head.

"And here's the biggest thing they changed, you see:
In MY story, I save Kai. Not he saves ME.
No prince's kiss. No act-of-true-love stuff.
I CRIED. My tears were warm. My tears were enough.

They melted ice. They washed the mirror out.
(There was a cursed mirror. That's what it's about —
A troll made a mirror that shows only bad.
A shard got in Kai's eye. It made him sad.)

I saved him with my tears. We walked home, holding hands.
We'd grown up on the journey. That's how it stands."
"So the original was about... growing up?" Elsa said.
Gerda nodded. "And friendship. Now go to bed.

You've got a sequel to worry about. And a dam."
"How do you know about the—" "I'm Gerda. I am
The girl who walks through anything. I know.
The Sámi were there first. Now let it go."
sections.What She Learned: That in Andersen's original, the HERO is Gerda — a brave girl who walks barefoot across the world to save her friend. That the Snow Queen is the villain, not the heroine. That there's a Sámi woman in the original 1844 story — the Frozen 2 connection goes all the way back. That Gerda saves Kai with tears, not a kiss. And that the story is about growing up and friendship, not romance or self-acceptance.
sections.Source Note: Hans Christian Andersen, 'Snedronningen' (The Snow Queen), 1844, Copenhagen. Seven chapters. The Sámi woman appears in Chapter 5 ('The Little Robber Girl'). Andersen called her 'the Lapp woman,' using the terminology of his era.

items.name: Miguel Dreams of the Lady of the Dead

sections.The Dreamscape: The marigold bridge from Coco — but halfway across, the orange petals turn to bone-white dust and the bridge descends into darkness. Not scary darkness. DEEP darkness, the way a seed feels deep in the soil. The neon-bright Land of the Dead fades into something older and quieter. A woman sits on a throne of bones, and she's not frightening at all. She's waiting.
sections.The Dream:
Miguel fell asleep beside his Mama Coco,
His guitar across his lap, a little loco,
And dreamed the marigold bridge beneath his feet
Turned into bones — a rhythmic, clicking beat.

The bones were MUSICAL. They clinked and clacked.
Each step he took, the bone-bridge answered back.
Down, down he went, past Pixar's neon town,
Into a darker place. But not a frown —

A DEEP place. Like the soil around a seed.
A woman sat there. Calm. No rush. No speed.
Her face was half a skull and half a smile.
"Sit down, mijo," she said. "Stay for a while."

"I'm... I'm Miguel. Are you... the Lady of the Dead?"
"Mictēcacīhuātl," she said. "But you can call me instead
Something shorter. Call me Mictē. I don't mind.
I've been down here a VERY long time. I've been kind.

I guard the bones." She held one up — a femur.
"The bones are seeds, you see. They're not a dreamer's
Nightmare. They're the opposite. They GROW.
Your great-great-grandpa Héctor? Now you know

His story — but I knew it first. I held his bones
Before your movie did. These are my zones."
"You're... older than Día de los Muertos?" Miguel gasped.
"Oh, MUCH older. Thousands of years have passed.

The Aztecs had a MONTH for death. Not two quick days.
The Spanish squished it down. That's how it plays
When someone conquers you — they shrink your grief
Into a weekend. But the underground belief

Survives. The marigolds? That's old. The ofrenda? OLD.
The sugar skulls? They're Aztec patterns, I am told.
La Catrina? She's my great-great-great-granddaughter,
Drawn by Posada, named by Rivera. Water

Flows downhill. Traditions flow through time.
Your movie got the FEELING right. The rhyme
Of living and dead, of memory and song,
Of how forgetting is the greatest wrong —

That's REAL. That's mine. That's what I've always known.
The dead aren't gone. They're seeds inside the bone."
Miguel strummed his guitar. A quiet chord.
"Remember me?" he sang. She smiled. "That word —

Remember — that's the oldest spell I've got.
Stronger than any curse, stronger than any plot.
You sing it for Mama Coco. I sing it for
Everyone who ever was. That's what I'm for."

He played, and in the bone-soil, deep below,
Something ancient stirred. Began to grow.
sections.What He Learned: That Día de los Muertos is much older than two days — the Aztecs celebrated for an entire month. That the Lady of the Dead (Mictēcacīhuātl) has been guarding bones for thousands of years. That bones are seeds in Nahua cosmology — the dead aren't gone, they're planted. That the ofrendas, marigolds, and sugar skulls survived the Spanish conquest. And that 'Remember Me' is the oldest spell in the world.
sections.Source Note: Mictēcacīhuātl from Aztec/Nahua mythology, recorded in the Codex Borgia and Sahagún's Florentine Codex (1576). La Catrina drawn by José Guadalupe Posada (c. 1910), named by Diego Rivera. Modern Día de los Muertos is a syncretic tradition blending Nahua and Catholic practices.

sections.The Dreamscape: A coffeehouse in old Aleppo — tiled walls, steam from tiny cups, the sound of a storyteller in the corner. Through one window: Disney's Agrabah, all purple domes and magic carpets. Through the other: actual 1709 Aleppo, busy with traders, smelling of cumin and wool. A young Syrian man sits cross-legged, gesturing wildly as he tells a tale.
sections.The Dream:
Aladdin fell asleep on his magic rug
And dreamed of coffee in a tiny mug.
A young man sat across from him. Brown eyes.
No lamp. No genie. No disguise.

"Who are you?" Aladdin asked, confused.
"I'm Hanna Diyab. And I'm NOT amused."
"About what?" "About YOU," the young man said.
"I MADE you up. Inside my HEAD.

I was eighteen! From Aleppo! I told a French guy
A story I invented on the fly
In Paris. 1709. He wrote it down.
He put it in a BOOK and spread it round

The entire WORLD. And never. Wrote. My. Name."
Aladdin blinked. "That's... someone else's blame?"
"It's GALLAND's blame. Antoine Galland. He took
My story, stuck it in his giant book —

'One Thousand and One Nights' — and said it was
From an 'ancient Arabic source,' BECAUSE
A Syrian kid in Paris isn't grand enough.
An ANCIENT MANUSCRIPT sounds like tougher stuff."

"Wait," said Aladdin. "You're saying I'm not ancient?"
"You're a teenager's campfire story! Patient,
Made-up, wonderful — but MINE. From Aleppo.
Not from 'Agrabah.' (That's not a place, fellow.)"

"But the Genie—" "The DJINN in my version? SCARY.
Not blue. Not funny. Definitely not merry.
Djinns are powerful spirits. We don't joke
About them. They're like fire — beautiful folk

Who burn you if you're careless." Hanna sipped
His coffee. "Also, I set it in China." Aladdin tripped.

"CHINA?!" "From Aleppo, China was the magical far-off land.
Like how Paris felt to me — you understand?
Every storyteller sets their tale
In the farthest place they know. Without fail."

"So I'm... a Syrian story set in China,
Made into a movie set in... diner—
I mean, Agrabah, which is nowhere real."
"Now you're getting it." Hanna grinned. "Here's the deal:

My city — Aleppo — was REAL. And old.
And beautiful. Silk Road traders, stories told
In coffeehouses just like this one here.
Christians, Muslims, Jews, all living near

Each other for a thousand years. THAT'S magic.
Not a cave of gold. The real thing's more... tragic
And more wonderful than any lamp could hold."
Aladdin looked at Hanna. "Can I be told

More stories? Real ones? From Aleppo?"
"That's all I ever wanted, fellow.
Someone to listen. Not a French translator
Who steals your name. Just... someone. Maybe later."

They sat and drank their coffee as the dream
Dissolved like sugar. Nothing's what things seem.
sections.What He Learned: That Aladdin was invented by a real person — Hanna Diyab, a young Syrian Christian from Aleppo. That Diyab told the story to a French translator who published it without credit. That the original was set in China, not Arabia. That Aleppo was a real, sophisticated, ancient city — not a fantasy 'Agrabah.' That djinns in the original tradition are powerful and frightening, not comic. And that the most magical thing about the Arabian Nights is that the most famous stories in it were made up by a teenager.
sections.Source Note: Hanna Diyab (c. 1688-c. 1763), Aleppo, Syria. Antoine Galland, 'Les Mille et une Nuits' (Paris, 1704-1717). Diyab's memoir rediscovered in Vatican Library. See Paulo Lemos Horta, 'Aladdin: A New Translation' (2019).

items.name: Mowgli Dreams of an Adivasi Child

sections.The Dreamscape: A forest — but which forest? One half is Disney-warm (bouncy branches, jazzy animals, a bear singing about necessities). The other half is a real Indian Sal forest: dappled light, actual bird calls (not orchestrated ones), the smell of damp leaves and cooking smoke from a nearby settlement. A child about Mowgli's age sits by a small fire, perfectly at home.
sections.The Dream:
Mowgli dreamed of trees. (He always did.)
But these trees didn't bounce. A real-life kid
Sat by a fire, roasting something small.
"What IS that?" Mowgli asked. "A forest ball?"

"It's a tuber," said the child. "A root. It's food.
I dug it up this morning. It's quite good."
"I'm Mowgli!" said the Disney boy. "The jungle's mine!"
The real child laughed. "The jungle's not a line

In a song. It's HOME. We've lived here — well,
Longer than your movie's tongue can tell."
"You live in the jungle? Like me? With wolves?"
"With my FAMILY," the child said. "Not with wolves."

"My name is—" but the wind blew, and the name
Was every name and no name, just the same
As any Baiga, Gond, or Bhil child born
In these forests since before the earliest morn.

"We don't call it 'jungle,'" said the child.
"That word means 'wild.' But this isn't wild.
We PLANTED some of these trees. My grandmother
Knows every herb. She's taught me and my brother

Which leaf cures belly-ache, which bark makes rope,
Which mushroom you can eat, which — nope, nope, nope.
The forest isn't chaos. It's a PLAN.
We've tended it since memory began."

"But Kipling said—" "Oh, KIPLING." The child sighed.
"An English man who watched from the outside.
He made the jungle into a SCHOOL for boys
Where animals teach rules. But the real joys

Are messier. A tiger isn't LAW.
A tiger's a tiger. With a paw
That kills your goat. We deal with it. We cope.
We don't PHILOSOPHIZE. We check the rope

Around the chicken coop. We bang a drum
To scare it off. (Tigers are kind of dumb
About loud noises.) And the wolves? They're fine.
They keep the deer population in a line."

"But I have to CHOOSE," said Mowgli, feeling sad.
"The jungle or the village. Good or bad."
"That's Kipling's choice," the child said, poking flame.
"We live in BOTH. That's not a losing game.

We farm at the forest edge. We gather inside.
The forest and the village are the SAME ride.
You don't choose between your left foot and your right.
You use them both to walk into the night."

Mowgli sat down. The fire was warm and small.
No bear was singing. No jazz. No monkey call.
Just crickets. And the real sound of a real place
Where a real child lived, with a real child's face.

"Can I stay?" asked Mowgli. "In this dream?"
"You can stay in any forest," said the gleam
Of firelight on the child's cheek, "if you listen.
Forests don't need boys. But they do glisten

When someone pays attention. Sit. Be still.
The forest speaks. It always has. It will."
sections.What He Learned: That the Adivasi peoples of India have lived in and tended forests for thousands of years — they're not 'in the wild,' they're HOME. That the forest isn't chaos (Kipling's 'jungle') but a carefully managed ecosystem. That you don't have to choose between the forest and the village — Adivasi communities live in both. That the Bishnoi community gave their lives protecting trees in 1730. And that a forest speaks if you listen — but it doesn't need a Disney soundtrack.
sections.Source Note: Adivasi communities across India, particularly the Baiga, Gond, and Bhil peoples of central India. The Bishnoi tree-hugging of 1730 (Khejarli massacre, Rajasthan). The Forest Rights Act of 2006, which recognizes Adivasi communities' ancestral relationship with Indian forests.

items.name: The Disney Versions: Bright, Shiny, and Missing Something

sections.About:
Eight Disney characters — each beloved, each iconic, each missing something essential. They share a pattern: Disney makes its characters more BEAUTIFUL, more ROMANTIC, and more INDIVIDUAL than the originals, while making them less COMPLEX, less CONNECTED to real communities, and less HONEST about how the world actually works.

Pocahontas is gorgeous but not ten years old. Ariel gets her voice back but never learns Andersen's lesson about the cost of wanting. Aurora sleeps beautifully but can't hold a sword. Moana finds herself but blurs thirty distinct Pacific cultures into one. Elsa lets it go but doesn't know Gerda walked barefoot to save a friend. Miguel remembers but doesn't know Mictēcacīhuātl has been guarding bones for millennia. Aladdin flies but doesn't know a Syrian teenager invented him. Mowgli bounces but doesn't know Adivasi children live in real forests without needing to choose between worlds.
sections.For Kids: These characters are great. You can love them. You SHOULD love them. But they're wearing costumes over older, realer people — and those people have better stories.

items.name: The Original Selves: Older, Stranger, and More Alive

sections.About:
Eight original selves — some real people, some mythological figures, some living communities. They share something the Disney versions lack: TEETH.

Amonute was kidnapped. The Sea-Foam Girl dissolved. Brynhildr killed her betrayer and chose her own death. The Wayfinders crossed the largest ocean on Earth by starlight. Gerda walked barefoot to the North Pole. Mictēcacīhuātl has been guarding the bones of the dead for three thousand years. Hanna Diyab invented the most famous story in the world and never got credit. Adivasi children live in forests that colonial governments tried to steal.

None of them needed a love interest. Most of them needed justice.
sections.For Kids: These are the people who were here before the movies. Some of them are still here. Some of them are your neighbors. All of them have stories that are bigger, weirder, and more wonderful than anything a movie can hold. Ask about them. Read about them. And if you meet someone from the Powhatan Nation, or Aleppo, or a Polynesian voyaging canoe — ask them to tell YOU the story. It'll be better than the dream.

items.name: The Dream Library: How to Use These Stories

sections.About:
Eight dream encounters in rhyme — designed to be read aloud to five-year-olds (and secretly enjoyed by the adults reading them).

Each dream follows the same pattern: a Disney character falls asleep and meets the real person or original character they were based on. The encounter is silly (because five-year-olds need silly), respectful (because real people deserve respect), and honest (because kids can handle truth if you put it in a poem).

The dreams are NOT 'corrections' — they don't say 'Disney is bad.' They say 'Disney is ONE version, and here's another, and the other one is older and stranger and worth knowing.'
sections.How to Read Them:
**Before watching**: Read the dream BEFORE watching the movie. It gives kids a frame: 'Let's see how different the movie is from the real story!'

**After watching**: Read the dream AFTER the movie as a conversation starter: 'Remember what the real Pocahontas said in the dream? She was only ten!'

**At bedtime**: These are bedtime stories. They're poems. They rhyme. They have a dreamy logic. Read them in a voice that's warm and a little silly.

**As a game**: After reading, ask your kid: 'If YOUR favorite character had a dream and met the real person, what would they say to each other?'

**The golden rule**: Don't use these to RUIN Disney. Use them to DEEPEN it. A kid who knows that Ariel's original dissolved into foam and that Aladdin was invented by a Syrian teenager has a richer imagination than a kid who only knows the movie. More stories = more magic, not less.
sections.Attribution Matters: Every dream poem includes a 'Source Note' crediting the original stories, traditions, and communities. Read these to your kids too. Names matter. 'A story from the Powhatan people' means more than 'an old story.' 'Invented by Hanna Diyab from Aleppo' means more than 'from the Arabian Nights.' Attribution is the opposite of erasure.
